comments.text: When conversing with ChatGPT on upper bounds on $T(n^k)$, it had the idea of using this 1983 paper by Adleman, Pomerance and Rumely (more precisely their Proposition $10$ on page $201$, which is page $29$ of the pdf-file) for a lower bound for specific values of $k$. To elaborate on this, for a given $k \ge 2$, let $Q$ be the product of all primes $p$ such that $p-1$ divides $k-1$. I then claim that $N := Q \lfloor Q^{k/2 - 1} \rfloor$ is not representable as a sum of $k$-th powers. If it were, note that we may only use elements from $A := \{1^k, 2^k, \ldots, \lfloor \sqrt{Q} \rfloor^k\}$, as $\lfloor \sqrt{Q} + 1\rfloor^k > N$. Furthermore, by the definition of $Q$ and Fermat's little theorem, we have $a^k \equiv a \pmod{Q}$ for all $a$. Hence, for any non-empty subset $S \subseteq A$ we have that the sum $\sum_{a \in S} a$, when reduced modulo $Q$, must be congruent to one of $\{1, 2, \ldots, \frac{\lfloor \sqrt{Q} \rfloor \lfloor \sqrt{Q} + 1 \rfloor}{2}\}$. As $Q \ge 2$, one can check that this latter set does not contain an element congruent to $N \equiv 0 \pmod{Q}$. In particular we deduce $T(n^k) > N > Q^{k/2} - Q$. Now, the aforementioned result by Adleman, Pomerance and Rumely (recently sharpened by Fan and Pollack here, and applied to $k-1$) shows that there exist infinitely many $k$ such that $Q$ has more than $e^{\frac{c \log k}{\log \log k}}$ prime factors. For such a $k$ we get $$T(n^k) > Q^{k/2} - Q > \left(2^{e^{\frac{c \log k}{\log \log k}}} \right)^{k/2} - Q > 2^{ke^{\frac{c' \log k}{\log \log k}}}.$$This grows slower than $2^{k^{1+\epsilon}}$ for any $\epsilon > 0$, but faster than $k^{Ck}$ for any $C$. Tldr; for every $C$ there exists a $k$ such that $T(n^k) > k^{Ck}$. So if we could somehow find a $C$ and infinitely many $k$ for which $T(n^k) < k^{Ck}$, then that would strongly suggest that the answer to the question at hand is affirmative.

I asked ChatGPT to optimize Kim's upper bound on $T(n^k)$ a little bit, and long story short is that here you can find a formalization of $$T(n^k) \le 2^{(3 + o(1))k^2}.$$One can compare this with Stijn's pigeonhole argument below, which gives $T(n^k) > k^k$. If it turns out to be the case that $T(n^k)$ is a monotonically increasing function of $k$, then one seemingly needs very good bounds on this function in order to be able to deduce this. That being said, because of the superexponential growth, an asymptotic would not be required. PS. The values of $T(n^k)$ in the remarks are off by $1$. E.g. $T(n^2) = 129$, as $128$ is the largest integer that cannot be written as the sum of distinct squares. PPS. Of course $T(n^k)$ is slight abuse of notation, but it's clear enough.

In the comment below, Stijn shows the lower bound $T(n^k) > 2^{c k \log k}$, which perhaps can be further improved if $k$ is a power of two. On the other hand, I wondered whether any upper bound was known and found this paper by Kim, where they make a proof by Graham explicit and find an upper bound of $T(n^k) < 2^{ck^4}$. Since this latter bound holds for all positive integers, plausibly it can be improved for specific values to see if $T(n^k) > T(n^{k+1})$ can be deduced for some $k$. Lower and upper bound are still quite far off from each other though. Kim, D. On the largest integer that is not a sum of distinct positive nth powers, Journal of Integer Sequences, Volume 20, Issue 7 (2017).

It may well that the answer is negative, i.e., that there aren't infinitely many such $k$ (if there is already even one of them). Background The value of $T(n^k)$ is known for $k\le 8$, see [oeis A001661]. In particular, we know that $T(n^8)<T(n^9)$ by the estimate by [Wiener23]. Remark on the growth of $T(x^k)$ and the candidates by EG The restrictedness is slightly larger; $n^{2^t} \equiv 0,1 \pmod{2^{t+2}}$ for every $t \ge 2$. This implies that indeed, when $r \equiv -1 \pmod{2^{t+2}}$, there are combinations with at least $2^{t+2}-1$ odd $2^t$th powers needed. From this, one gets immediately a lowerbound for $T(n^{2^t})$ equal to $$\sum_{i=1}^{2^{t+2}-1}(2i-1)^{2^t}=(c+o(1)) \int_1^{2^{t+3}} x^{2^{t}} dx=(c+o(1))*2^{(t+3)2^t+3}$$ for some $0.375<c<1/2$. But one can actually do a better estimate. Let $n$ be the solution to $n^k=2^n$, so $$n=\log_2(e) k (\ln(k)+\ln(\ln(k))+o(1))=k (\log_2(k)+\log_2(\log_2(k)+O(1)).$$ Now, since there are only $2^{n-1}$ combinations of $1^k$ up to $(n-1)^k$, there is definitely a number in the range $[2^{n-1},2^n]$ which cannot be represented as the sum of different $k^{th}$ powers. Thus $T(x^k)>2^{n-1}=2^{ k (\log_2(k)+\log_2(\log_2(k))+O(1))}.$ When $k=2^t$, we notice that this is of the form $\Theta\left(2^{ 2^t(t+\log_2(t))} \right)$, which is larger than $\Theta \left( 2^{(t+3)2^t}\right)$. The presented lower bound is far off from the correct bound, but also grows by a factor which is at least $ek \log_2(k).$
